content:
An incremental Add-to-App migration of a production consumer-finance app: the decisions, the trade-offs, and the code that makes two worlds share one session.

9 min read
Just now

--

Two years ago, our team was shipping the same feature twice. Every credit-application flow, every payment screen, every regulatory change landed in two backlogs: once in Swift, once in Kotlin. Two codebases, two teams, two review pipelines — and, inevitably, two slightly different apps pretending to be one product.
This series is about how we’re fixing that — and it’s not the way most migration stories go. There was no rewrite. There was no feature freeze. We’re moving a production consumer-finance app — over a million installs, operating under national financial-regulatory oversight, handling credit applications, collateral-based loan disbursement, installment payments, and a rewards-and-commerce ecosystem — to Flutter one feature at a time, with Flutter embedded inside the existing native apps. Users kept transacting through every release. Most will never know a migration happened.
This is written for engineers who already ship mobile apps. I won’t explain what a widget is. I will, over the course of this series, explain why we run two routing systems in one module on purpose.
Where we’re going
Most Flutter Add-to-App examples focus on embedding Flutter into an existing app. Our challenge began after Flutter was already running. We weren’t integrating a single feature — we were planning a months-long migration of an entire production application.
Instead of asking “How do we launch Flutter from native?”, we asked a different question:
“How do we make Flutter behave like just another native feature while the migration is still in progress?”
That single question shaped almost every architectural decision that followed. The result was an architecture that looks surprisingly different from most Add-to-App examples.
Before we explain why each decision was made, here’s the overall shape of what we built. Every piece below is a deliberate trade-off — none of it is how the standard Add-to-App tutorial does it.

• One Flutter gateway instead of dozens of Flutter modules. Most Add-to-App examples create a Flutter module for each integration point. We deliberately built a single gateway capable of serving every migrated flow. That decision simplified versioning, reduced integration work, and eventually became the backbone of the migration.

• Flutter is consumed like any other internal SDK. Native engineers don’t clone the Flutter repository or install the Flutter SDK. They simply update a Maven dependency. If a Flutter release goes wrong, rolling back is a one-line version change.

• Every migrated flow starts with a fresh Flutter engine. Rather than keeping one long-lived engine alive for the entire app, we create isolated engines from a shared FlutterEngineGroup, each launched into a custom Dart entrypoint instead of main().

• Crossing the native–Flutter boundary doesn’t interrupt the user’s journey. Authentication tokens, device identity, and cryptographic keys move across the platform boundary, allowing users to continue their existing session without ever seeing another login screen.

• The migration already contains its own endgame. The Flutter module isn’t just embedded inside the native app — it is designed to eventually become the app itself. When the final native screen disappears, the architecture doesn’t change. The host does.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Part 1 covers why we chose this path at all. The architecture above is what Parts 2 and 3 unpack — and, because no honest migration story is complete without them, the gotchas, including the hard-coded delay(1500) we're not proud of.
The problem: one product, two apps
On paper we had one app. In practice we had two mature, diverging products — and the failure mode wasn’t dramatic. It was drift.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

• Feature divergence. A flow would ship on Android first; iOS would catch up a release or two later — or implement it subtly differently. Support tickets started including the question “which phone do you have?”

• Double maintenance. One product requirement meant two implementations, two sets of edge cases, two QA passes.

• Compliance amplification. In a regulated finance product, an auditor’s finding isn’t fixed until it’s fixed on both platforms. Every security patch had double the surface area and double the verification burden.

• Team economics. Two platform teams, two rare skill sets to hire and retain — and the effective feature throughput of one team.
None of this was urgent on any given day. Compounded across a monthly release cadence, it was the single biggest tax on the product: every initiative implicitly priced at 2×, and feature parity a permanent project rather than a property of the codebase.
The rules we couldn’t break
The fix seems obvious — one cross-platform codebase, one implementation per feature, parity by construction. But “rewrite the app” is a very different proposition when the app is a bank-adjacent product with a million users mid-loan. Three constraints were non-negotiable:

• No user-facing disruption. People have active loans, due dates, and payment autodebits in this app. “Please reinstall and log in again” was not an acceptable release note.

• No feature freeze. The business would not pause a competitive lending product for 12–18 months. The migration had to happen while monthly releases continued.

• No compliance regression. Secure storage, device binding, liveness checks, audit trails — everything the regulator had already reviewed had to keep working, unchanged, throughout.
These constraints ruled out the big-bang rewrite before we ever compared frameworks. Whatever we chose had to coexist with the native apps for months on end — so the real evaluation criterion wasn’t “which framework is best?” but “which framework is best at living inside an existing native app?”
What we were starting from
It’s worth being concrete about the starting point, because the maturity of these codebases shaped every decision that follows.
This wasn’t legacy spaghetti waiting for an excuse to be rewritten. Both apps were healthy, modular, and actively shipped — which is exactly what made the duplication so expensive. Here’s an abbreviated view of the Android settings.gradle.kts; every module below had a Swift twin in a different repo, owned by a different team:
// settings.gradle.kts — the Android half of "one product, two apps"
include(":feature:login")
include(":feature:onboarding")
include(":feature:home")
include(":feature:apply-limit")
include(":feature:payment")
include(":feature:transaction")
include(":feature:marketplace")
include(":feature:renewal")
include(":feature:inbox")
include(":feature:faq")
// ... ~40 feature modules and ~35 data modules in total —
// and a complete second implementation of all of it in the iOS repoEvery feature here was designed once and built twice, by engineers who didn’t share a language, a toolchain, or a sprint board. That’s the codebase we had to migrate — without anyone noticing.
Why Flutter
We evaluated four options seriously: stay native, Kotlin Multiplatform, React Native, and Flutter.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Staying native was the honest baseline: maximum platform fidelity, zero migration risk — and the 2× tax locked in permanently. It meant solving an organizational problem with process instead of architecture. We’d already tried that; drift won.
Kotlin Multiplatform appealed to the Android half of the org: shared business logic, fully native UI. But that’s precisely its limitation for us — UI was where most of our duplication lived. KMP would have shared the layer we duplicated least and kept separate the layer we duplicated most, while making the iOS team dependent on a toolchain owned by the Android side. (At decision time, Compose Multiplatform wasn’t yet viable for sharing UI on iOS; evaluate it fresh if you’re reading this later.)
React Native was the strongest competitor on paper — bigger talent pool, mature ecosystem. Three things tipped us to Flutter:

• Whose talent pool. Our migration would be carried by the engineers who already knew the domain, the regulator’s expectations, and a decade of edge cases — Kotlin and Swift developers. For them, Dart is a short hop: sound static types, AOT compilation, familiar syntax. A JS/TS toolchain is a different world. The question wasn’t “which framework is easier to hire for?” but “which framework can our existing native teams become productive in fastest?”

• Rendering ownership. The entire point of the migration was ending platform divergence — and RN renders through platform components, so a slice of per-platform behavior survives by design. Flutter draws every pixel itself, which makes UI parity a property of the framework rather than a QA goal. The same ownership mattered for compliance-critical flows: liveness detection over live camera preview, OCR overlays, secure input. (At decision time RN’s New Architecture was still maturing; JSI/Fabric has since addressed several old bridge criticisms — re-evaluate if you’re deciding today.)

• The embedding story — decisive for an incremental migration. Flutter’s Add-to-App is a first-class workflow: flutter build aar / XCFramework produce versioned artifacts, FlutterEngineGroup manages engines, and native can launch custom Dart entrypoints. RN can be embedded, but the pipeline we needed — module as a versioned artifact, published to Maven, pinned per environment — is largely something you assemble yourself. We'd live in hybrid mode for months; embedding quality mattered more than greenfield quality.
The trade-offs we accepted
We chose Flutter with our eyes open — we hit every one of these:

• Binary size. The Flutter engine adds real megabytes to apps that were already large. For an iOS app around 330 MB, that was a genuine cost we accepted, not a rounding error.

• Memory. Every running engine has a footprint. Our design spins up a fresh engine from a shared FlutterEngineGroup each time a user enters a migrated flow and destroys it on exit — trading startup cost and memory churn for state isolation. You feel that trade on low-end Android devices, which for a lending product in our market is a meaningful share of users. (The full engine lifecycle is in Part 3.)

• Two hiring pools during transition. For as long as the migration runs — months of hybrid releases — we need native experts and Flutter engineers. The team-cost savings arrive at the end, not the beginning. Budget accordingly.

• The bridge is a tax. Every capability that stays native — biometrics, secure storage, payment SDKs, push — becomes a platform-channel contract to design, version, and test on both sides. Add-to-App doesn’t remove native work; it concentrates it into an interface.
The trade-offs were the ones our constraints could absorb — and Flutter was the only option where “embed inside two production apps and migrate feature-by-feature” felt like a supported path rather than a hack.
What’s next
Choosing Flutter turned out to be the easy part. The real challenge was making Flutter disappear.
Users shouldn’t notice when they move from Kotlin or Swift into Dart. QA shouldn’t need a separate testing strategy because a screen happens to be written in Flutter. Native engineers shouldn’t need to care whether the next feature comes from a native module or a Flutter module.
Those principles became the foundation for every engineering decision that followed. The rest of the series unpacks them in two parts:
Part 2 — The strategy and the architecture.

• Why we built a single Flutter Gateway instead of one module per feature — shipped to native teams as a versioned Maven artifact, no Flutter SDK required.

• What happens to native code once its Flutter replacement ships — and how the module was designed from day one to eventuall
